Let us repeat once more the methods by which we can increase our combinative skill: (1) by careful examination of the different types and by a clear understanding of their motives and their premises, (2) By memorising a number of outstanding as well as of common examples and solutions, (3) Frequent repetition (in thought, if possible) of important combinations, so as to develop the imagination. Not allowing the king to get to relative safety on White needed to avoid 29.¬£e4? ¬£f1‚Ä†‚Äď+ and with the help of some checks, Black manages to trade queens, with a winning material advantage. 29...¬¶bxe8 29...¬¶hxe8 does not help either: 30.g6‚Ä† ¬Ęf8 31.¬£f4‚Ä† (or similarly 31.¬£f3‚Ä† or 31.¬£f2‚Ä†) 31...¬Ęg8 32.¬£f7‚Ä† ¬Ęh8 33.¬¶xe8‚Ä† ¬¶xe8 34.¬£xe8‚Ä† ¬•f8 35.¬£xf8 mate ‚úď 30.g6‚Ä† ¬Ęg8 31.¬¶xe8‚Ä† ‚úď 1‚Äď0 The attack decides.

gameSource: Anatoly Karpov ‚Äď Miguel Quinteros, Buenos Aires 1980
comment: The g-file is where the weaknesses are, so White forcefully attacks them. Not 32.¬•h5? ¬•d3‚Äď+ and Black defends. 32...¬¶xh7 32...exf5 33.¬•xf5+‚Äď ‚úď (or 33.¬•f3+‚Äď) 33.fxg6‚Ä† 33.¬¶xh7 exf5 34.¬•xf5+‚Äď is just as strong. 33...¬Ęg8 33...¬Ęxg6 34.¬•xe6‚Ä† ¬£g5 35.¬§f4 mate and 33...¬Ęe8 34.gxh7+‚Äď lose as well. 34.gxh7‚Ä† ¬Ęh8 35.¬§f4! 1‚Äď0 The g-file is still the target.

Opening up the black king. 34...cxd5 34...exd5 35.¬£f5‚Ä†+‚Äď ‚úď with a winning attack. 35.c6‚Ä†! ¬Ęxc6 35...bxc6 36.¬¶xb8+‚Äď ‚úď 36.¬£b5‚Ä† 1‚Äď0 White picks up the rook on g1: 36...¬Ęd6 37.¬£b6‚Ä† ¬Ęe7 38.¬£xg1+‚Äď ‚úď

Exploiting the pin to get at the weak c4-pawn. Black will win this pawn with a serious advantage. 17.¬§d4 17.¬§xe7‚Ä† ¬£xe7‚Äď+ ‚úď and the pawn is still doomed. 17.¬£c2 ¬§xc4! ‚úď 18.¬£xc4 ¬§xd5‚Äď+ (or 18...¬•xd5‚Äď+); 17.bxa5 ¬¶xb1‚Äď+ ‚úď 17...¬§xc4‚Äď+ ‚úď (0‚Äď1, 25 moves)

and White soon won. Other moves are also good enough. 32...¬£c8 and other passive queen moves allow White to attack on the long diagonal. Even stronger is starting with 33.h5. 33.¬£d4+‚Äď ‚úď A double threat, winning the bishop.

Queen and knight is the usual combo, but here a queen with a bishop wreaks havoc when the king‚Äôs defending pawns are gone. The fact that the knight on d5 only protects dark squares plays a large part in giving such a free rein to the bishop. Good enough only for a clear advantage are 31.¬£a4 and 31.¬£c4. 31...¬Ęxf7 32.¬£xg6‚Ä† ¬Ęf8 32...¬Ęe7 33.¬£g7‚Ä† ¬Ęe8 34.¬•g6 mate ‚úď 33.¬£xh6‚Ä† 1‚Äď0 Black resigned due to 33...¬Ęe8 34.¬•b5‚Ä†+‚Äď ‚úď and 33...¬Ęg8 34.¬•h7‚Ä† ¬Ęf7 35.¬•g6‚Ä† ¬Ęg8 36.¬£h7‚Ä† ¬Ęf8 37.¬£f7 mate.

gameSource: Anatoly Karpov ‚Äď Gian Carlo Angioni, Turin (simul) 1982
comment: White is much better after retreating the queen, but winning outright is the way to go. 36...¬¶xd5 36...¬Ęh8 37.¬§f7‚Ä† ¬Ęg8 38.¬§d8‚Ä† (or 38.¬§e5‚Ä†+‚Äď) 38...¬¶xd5 39.¬£f7‚Ä† ¬Ęh8 40.¬£e8‚Ä† ¬•f8 41.¬£xf8 mate ‚úď 37.¬£f7‚Ä† ¬Ęh8 38.¬£xd5 ‚úď White is clearly winning and the game ended immediately: 38...¬¶e8 39.¬§f7‚Ä† 1‚Äď0 Mate is coming.

A common sacrifice in the French, but here it would have been better to abstain from it. 19...g5! is the only move that‚Äôs not clearly worse for Black. White is slightly better after 20.¬•xe4 gxf4 ‚úď 21.¬•d3 fxg3 22.fxg3!. 20.gxf4 ¬£xf4 21.¬•xe4! dxe4 22.¬§e5! Activity brings White a large advantage. 22...¬§xe5 22...¬£g5‚Ä† 23.¬Ęh1¬Ī 23.dxe5 ¬•d7 24.¬£xd7 ¬¶f8 24...¬£g4‚Ä† 25.¬Ęf1 ¬£h3‚Ä† 26.¬Ęe2 ¬£f3‚Ä† 27.¬Ęd2 ¬£f8 28.¬Ęc1!+‚Äď 25.¬£d1+‚Äď 25.¬£d4+‚Äď 25...¬£g5‚Ä† 26.¬Ęh1 ¬¶xf2 27.¬¶g1 1‚Äď0

Black missed a great opportunity for a serious upset against the reigning world champion. Instead the game went 28...¬§xg3? 29.hxg3 ¬£xg3 30.¬¶xf5+‚Äď (1‚Äď0, 36 moves). 29.¬Ęxh2 ¬§xg3 29...¬•xg3‚Ä†? 30.¬Ęg1+‚Äď 30.¬£b5 There is no rescue after 30.¬£a6 ¬§e2‚Ä† ‚úď , mating on the h-file. 30...¬§e2‚Ä†‚Äď+ ‚úď White is forced to give back the queen, leaving Black with a winning position. 30...¬¶h6‚Ä† 31.¬Ęg1 cxb5 also wins.
1‚Äď0 White picks up a lot of material for the queen, gaining a winning material advantage.

Threatening mate. 35...¬£e5 36.¬¶g8‚Ä†! 36.¬£xe5? ¬§xe5 37.¬¶xa6 d2 gives Black serious counterplay with the d-pawn. 36...¬Ęe7 37.d6‚Ä†! ‚úď Picking up the queen with a winning attack. The game finished: 37...¬Ęe6 37...¬£xd6 38.¬§f5‚Ä† is mating. 38.¬¶e8‚Ä† ¬Ęd5 39.¬¶xe5‚Ä† ¬§xe5 40.d7 ¬¶b8 41.¬§xf7 1‚Äď0

and Black has some drawing chances after 40...¬•xf2‚Ä†¬Ī. 37...¬§d6 No salvation is offered by 37...¬Ęg7 38.¬§xf7+‚Äď ‚úď or 37...¬•e7 38.c7+‚Äď. 38.¬§xf7! ¬§xf7 38...¬§xb7 39.¬§xd8 ¬§xd8 (39...¬§d6 40.¬§e6‚Ä†+‚Äď ‚úď ) 40.c7+‚Äď ‚úď White has a new queen on the way. 39.c7 ‚úď ¬¶e8 40.¬•d7! Winning the most material. 40.¬¶b8! is also good: 40...¬¶a8 41.¬¶xa8 ¬¶xa8 42.¬¶xc5+‚Äď The game continuation of 40.c8=¬£?! ¬¶xc8 41.¬•xc8 is probably winning, but also not totally clear (1‚Äď0, 60 moves). 40...¬§d6 41.¬¶b8!+‚Äď

gameSource: Anatoly Karpov ‚Äď Lars Bo Hansen, Thessaloniki (ol) 1988
comment: Picking up a pawn. 20.g4 is also quite strong and if you evaluated 20...¬£e7 21.¬§xf7 as winning, you get full points. 20...¬£d4 20...¬Ęxf7 21.¬¶c7‚Ä† ¬Ęf8 22.¬¶xb7+‚Äď ‚úď The check on d1 is not dangerous. 21.¬£d6! Clearly best, but not necessary to see before taking on f7. White tries to get the knight out with tempo, and trade off Black‚Äôs active queen, but he also attacks the weak pawns. 21...¬£b2 22.¬¶f1+‚Äď 22.¬¶d1+‚Äď is also good enough. Keeping everything protected is best tactically here. The game ended in a few moves: 22...¬¶e8 23.¬£c7 ¬•a8 24.¬§g5 h6 25.¬£f7‚Ä† ¬Ęh8 26.e5 1‚Äď0

is the best defence, but Black is winning with an extra pawn and an ongoing attack. After the game move 31.¬£xa7‚Äď+, Black‚Äôs position is totally winning due to the weak white king (0‚Äď1, 42 moves). 31...¬£xb2‚Ä†‚Äď+ ‚úď Winning the rook. But not 31...¬¶d2‚Ä†? 32.¬Ęe1¬Ī.

Using the fact that the queen is bound to the defence of g3. 22.¬£f3 a) 22.¬¶f5!? ¬•xd3 23.¬¶xg5 h2‚Ä† 24.¬Ęh1 f5! ‚úď and ...¬•e4‚Ä† will decide the game. b) Even quicker is 22.¬£xb5 ¬£xg3‚Ä† 23.¬Ęh1 ¬£g2 mate ‚úď . 22...¬•xf1‚Äď+ ‚úď

Surprisingly counterattacking the cluster of white pieces in the centre. 25.¬•f3 e5! 26.dxe5 Alternatives are easy to dismiss: 26.hxg6 exf4‚Äď+ ‚úď and 26.¬•c1 e4‚Äď+ ‚úď . 26...¬£xd3‚Äď+ ‚úď (0‚Äď1, 30 moves)

Instead, Black gained a winning attack after: 36.¬¶f1 ¬¶xf1‚Ä† 37.¬Ęxf1 ¬£xh2‚Äď+ ‚úď Moves that carry a threat, so that taking on h2 can be played soon, are also winning. 38.¬¶d5 ¬•xd5 39.cxd5 ¬£xg3 40.fxg5 ¬£f3‚Ä† 0‚Äď1 36...¬¶xe1‚Ä† 37.¬¶f1 ¬£f3!! All-out attack with the pieces! 38.¬¶xe1 ¬£h1‚Ä† 39.¬Ęf2 ¬£g2‚Ä† 40.¬Ęe3 ¬£f3 mate ‚úď

A well-known tactical theme in this type of position, using rook against queen. 14...¬§fxd5 14...¬§bxd5 15.¬§xd5 (15.¬•xd5? ¬§xd5¬Ķ) 15...¬•xd5 16.¬•xd5 ¬§xd5 17.¬•xe7 ¬£xe7 18.¬¶xd5+‚Äď 15.¬§xd5 Or 15.¬•xe7, but not 15.¬•xd5? ¬§xd5 16.¬•xe7 ¬§xc3¬Ķ. 15...¬•xg5 16.¬§xb4+‚Äď ‚úď The game ended after the further: 16...¬£e7 17.¬§d5 ¬•xd5 18.¬•xd5 1‚Äď0 White is simply a piece up for a pawn.

Threatening a fork on g5 while simultaneously cutting off the rook from the defence of the e3-pawn. 31...¬•xc5?! allows enough counterplay for a draw: 32.¬¶e8‚Ä† ¬¶xe8 33.¬£xe8‚Ä† ¬£g8 (33...¬Ęg7? 34.¬£e5‚Ä† ¬Ęh6 35.¬£xc5+‚Äď) 34.¬£e5‚Ä† ¬£g7 35.¬£e8‚Ä†= Also equal is: 31...¬§b1?! 32.h3. 32.h4 32.¬¶e1 ¬§g5‚Äď+ ‚úď 32...¬£xe3‚Ä†‚Äď+ ‚úď 33.¬Ęh1 ¬£d4 0‚Äď1

Pulling the rook to a dangerous square. 26...f4?! 27.g4= 27.¬¶xd3 f4 A double threat against d3 and g3. 28.g4 ¬£xd3 29.gxh5 White has enough material, but the pin on the second rank decides. 29...¬¶e2! 30.¬£h4 ¬¶xg2‚Ä† 30...h6 creates luft and soon wins on g3/f3. 31.¬Ęxg2 ¬£xf3‚Ä† 32.¬Ęh2 ¬£g2 mate

The white knight uses the immobility of the black queen and knight to head for a5, b6 or d6, whichever has the deadliest effect. 27...¬¶b8 Three alternatives: a) 27...¬£c7 28.¬£xb7 (or 28.¬§d6‚Ä†+‚Äď) 28...¬£xb7 29.¬§d6‚Ä†+‚Äď ‚úď ; b) 27...¬£xc4 28.¬£xb7+‚Äď ‚úď ; c) 27...0‚Äď0 28.¬§a5+‚Äď ‚úď 28.¬§xb6 0‚Äď0 Black also has options here: 28...¬£xb6 29.¬£xb6 ¬¶xb6 30.¬¶a8‚Ä†+‚Äď ‚úď and 28...¬¶xb6 29.¬£a8‚Ä†+‚Äď ‚úď . 29.¬§c4+‚Äď ‚úď (1‚Äď0, 32 moves) 29.¬§a8+‚Äď is more convoluted, but should work as well.
transposes or wins material straight away. 20...¬Ęxg7 21.¬§f5‚Ä† exf5 22.¬¶xe7 ¬§xe7 23.¬£e2!+‚Äď ‚úď The double attack wins a piece.

comment: is equal, for example 36...¬§xf3‚Ä† 37.¬Ęg2 ¬§h4‚Ä†=, and 36.¬¶xg7‚Ä†? ¬Ęxg7 37.¬•xh6‚Ä† ¬Ęxh6 simply loses for White. 36...¬§f5 36...f5 is met by the same theme as in the game. 37.¬¶xg7‚Ä†! 1‚Äď0 Black foresaw 37...¬Ęxg7 38.¬•xh6‚Ä† ¬Ęxh6 39.¬£xd1+‚Äď ‚úď .

All White‚Äôs pieces are aimed at a very lonely black king. 1‚Äď0 Karpov didn‚Äôt want to see 26...¬Ęg8 27.¬•xg7! with the classic double-bishop sacrifice and a neat finish: 27...¬Ęxg7 (after 27...f5 many moves lead to mate, for instance 28.¬£g6+‚Äď) 28.¬¶g3‚Ä† ¬Ęf6 29.¬£g5 mate ‚úď

Attacking the defending bishop. 31.¬¶h8‚Ä† ¬Ęf7 only gives a clear advantage and 31.¬•d4? ¬¶e1‚Ä† 32.¬Ęa2 ¬£xc2‚Äď+ is even weaker. 31...¬¶e7 A desperate attempt to prolong the game, but the outcome should not be in question. But it still offers more hope than 31...¬•xc5 32.¬£xg7 mate ‚úď or 31...¬¶e1‚Ä† 32.¬Ęa2 ¬£xc2 33.¬¶h8‚Ä† ¬Ęf7 34.¬¶xf8 mate ‚úď . 32.¬•xe7+‚Äď (1‚Äď0, 74 moves) Other moves are winning too.

29...¬§ge4‚Äď+ ‚úď Instead, Black let White into the game with 29...¬¶xe3? 30.fxe3 ¬§ge4¬≥ and lost in 47 moves. Trading rooks reduces the impact of the previously free d-pawn and gives the white pieces more active possibilities.
basically admitted defeat (0‚Äď1, 46 moves). 25.¬£xg3 ¬¶xb5 ‚úď also loses. 25...¬£b6‚Ä† 26.¬Ęf1 ¬£xb5‚Äď+ ‚úď

Or 17.¬¶xg7‚Ä† ¬Ęxg7 18.¬§xf5‚Ä† transposing. 17...¬¶xf5 18.¬¶xg7‚Ä†! ¬Ęxg7 19.¬£g4‚Ä†+‚Äď ‚úď White wins back the rook, with an extra piece. 19...¬Ęf6 20.¬§e4‚Ä† Best, but there are other winning continuations as well. 20...¬Ęe5 21.¬§g3 1‚Äď0 Or 21.¬£g3‚Ä† ¬Ęxe4 22.¬£g4‚Ä† ¬Ęe5 23.¬£d4 mate.

comment: 39.¬£xe6 ‚úď 1‚Äď0 White wins f7 with a killing attack. The biggest problem I see among people who want to excel in chess ‚Äď and in business and in life in general ‚Äď is not trusting their instincts enough.

and White only wins an exchange, while 24.¬•h6?? even loses: 24...¬¶e1‚Ä† 25.¬•f1 ¬•a6‚Äď+ Instead 24.¬§h6‚Ä† ¬Ęh8 25.¬£f5 g6 26.¬§xf7‚Ä†? ¬Ęg7 27.¬£d7 looks clever, but Black has 27...¬•c8‚Äď+. 24...¬•xg7 24...¬•c8 25.¬§e6‚Ä† ¬Ęh8 26.¬£f5 also leads to mate. 25.¬•h6 ‚úď 1‚Äď0

The critical test. The game instead saw 27...¬£b1‚Ä† 28.¬Ęg2+‚Äď ‚úď when White has a winning attack. Black resigned after 28...¬•xe5 29.¬§c3. 28.¬§f4‚Ä† ¬Ęf5 29.¬¶xf7‚Ä† ¬Ęg5 30.¬§xe2+‚Äď ‚úď White is simply a piece up.

gameSource: Garry Kasparov ‚Äď Comp Meph Exclusive S, Hamburg (simul) 1985
comment: Moving the king is the only way to avoid the mate on g7, but Black is mated all the same after: 19.¬•e4 ‚úď 1‚Äď0

wins the knight on c3, but doesn‚Äôt mate on b2: 27.¬Ęb1 ¬£xc3 28.¬¶xd5+‚Äď However, 24...¬¶b4?! is better for Black. Without the second pair of rooks, White will not have enough compensation for the exchange. 25.b3 Giving up the b2- pawn with, for instance, 25.¬¶1d3 might be a better idea, but Black is much better. And he wins after 25.¬¶xb4? ¬•xe3‚Ä† 26.¬Ęb1 ¬¶xb4. ‚úď 25...e5! Overloading the queen. 26.¬£xe5 ¬£xc3‚Äď+ ‚úď
is mating. 39...¬¶e8! ‚úď 0‚Äď1 Winning the bishop, and thus the game.

comment: 37...¬¶xb3 ‚úď 0‚Äď1 Black is a piece up, and winning.

The white queen is in the crosshairs of the black queen and bishop. The rook cannot be taken due to the further pin by the bishop, and the knight cannot take the bishop since the queen would drop. And finally a queen trade would allow the intermediate ...¬•xf2‚Ä† winning a rook. Instead the game continued 35...¬£xe3 36.¬§xe3 ¬•d4 37.¬¶xd2 ¬•xe3‚Ä† 38.¬Ęh2 ¬•xd2 39.gxf3 ¬¶g3!¬Ķ and Black managed to convert his advantage, although things are far from over. 36.¬£xg5 Other moves also lose immediately: 36.¬£xd4‚Ä† ¬¶xd4‚Äď+ ‚úď , 36.¬§xd4 ¬£xe3‚Äď+ ‚úď and 36.¬£xd2 ¬£xg2 mate ‚úď . 36...¬•xf2‚Ä†‚Äď+ ‚úď
and the back-rank mate decides. 37...¬¶xc3 38.¬£xb4 Instead White resigned after 38.¬¶d4 ¬£d6 39.f6 ¬•xf6 since 40.¬£e8‚Ä† does not mate. 38...¬•xb4‚Äď+ ‚úď

Black cannot resist the white attack since he has no defenders against so many attackers. 18.¬•xg5! 18.exf6?! ¬•xf6 19.¬•xh6 ¬•xd4! is rather unclear. 18...fxg5 19.¬¶f6! ‚úď The weak f6-square is Black‚Äôs downfall. There are several ways to win from here by playing stuff like ¬¶af1 and ¬¶xh6, as in the game, or simply opening up the kingside with h4. 19...¬Ęg7 20.¬¶af1 White threatens, among others, 21.¬¶xh6 ¬Ęxh6 22.¬¶f6‚Ä† ¬£xf6 23.exf6 ¬¶g8 24.¬£e3 followed by ¬•d3 and ¬£h3. 20...¬§e7 21.h4 1‚Äď0 Black cannot resist the attack.

Threatening mate on a7. The idea is to force Black‚Äôs queen to h2, so he can‚Äôt take on b6 in case of a5-a6. 30.¬Ęf1 gives a clear advantage and 30.¬¶e5 ¬£c6 31.¬•f3+‚Äď is a complicated win ‚Äď due to zugzwang! 30...¬£h2‚Ä† 30...¬¶xe8 31.¬£xh6+‚Äď ‚úď 31.¬Ęf1 ¬¶xe8 31...¬£xg2‚Ä† 32.¬Ęxg2 d4‚Ä† 33.¬£xb7‚Ä†! (33.f3 ¬¶xe8 34.a6 also wins, as does 33.¬¶e4 ¬•xe4‚Ä† 34.f3 and 33.¬Ęf1 ¬¶xe8 34.a6.) 33...¬¶xb7 34.¬¶xh8+‚Äď 32.a6 ‚úď 1‚Äď0 White‚Äôs point, mating.
